target.name: Sunfire
target.founder: Carl Berninghausen, Nils Aldag, Christian von Olshausen

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $315M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Sunfire is a Series E climate and energy technology company founded by Carl Berninghausen, Nils Aldag, and Christian von Olshausen, having raised $315M+ to date. The sector peer universe includes one public comparable (LNZA) and eight private companies, including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen.

Valuation analysis relies primarily on stage-progression methodology, as a narrowly-filtered peer median approach proved inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within the Climate & Energy sector. While nine same-sector peers exist, none met the threshold of three or more datapoints when filtered for stage and valuation tier proximity. The stage-progression method applies a 1.4x step-up multiplier to the $315M total raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $441M.

The triangulated valuation range is $375M (low) to $507M (high), with a median point estimate of $441M. This single-method result carries elevated uncertainty given the reliance on historical stage-step multiples rather than direct peer comparables. Key uncertainties include the appropriateness of the 1.4x multiplier for Sunfire's specific risk and market conditions, and the broader absence of tight-fit public or late-stage private comparables in the climate technology space. Additional diligence on recent capital deployment, unit economics, and relative market positioning within the climate-energy ecosystem would refine this range.
